statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, perform a max-heapify insertion at the specified index and return the resulting array. However, note that the provided implementation has a subtle bug that leads to incorrect results for certain inputs.
statement.inputFormat: Input is an array of integers and an index into the array.
statement.outputFormat: The modified array after performing max-heapify insertion at the specified index.
statement.notes: The implementation must maintain the max-heap property for all indices in the array.

examples.explanation: The array [2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7] is initially a max-heap. After performing max-heapify insertion at index 3, the resulting array should be [6, 7, 5, 4, 3, 2].

hints.text: The bug in the implementation lies in the handling of the parent node during max-heapify insertion.

editorial.title: Max Heapify Insertion
editorial.intuition: The key to solving this problem is to correctly update the parent node during max-heapify insertion.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach would involve recursively checking the max-heap property for all nodes in the array after the insertion.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using the heapify-up operation to correctly update the parent node during max-heapify insertion.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness involves showing that the heap property is maintained after the max-heapify insertion operation.
editorial.dryRun: Performing a dry run with the array [2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7] and index 3 will help illustrate the correct approach.

functionSignature.cpp: int* maxHeapifyInsertion(int* nums, int n, int index);
functionSignature.java: int[] maxHeapifyInsertion(int[] nums, int n, int index);
functionSignature.python: def max_heapify_insertion(self, nums: list[int], n: int, index: int) -> list[int]:
